That is why Antigravity's primary "Editor view" is a state-of-the-art AI-powe

Eclipse IDE is a leading open platform for professional developers.

파악됐어요. 제가 완전히 잘못 알고 있었네요. AntiGravity는 Google이 2025년 11월에 출시한 **agent-first AI IDE**예요 — VS Code 기반 포크에 Gemini 3 Pro를 기본 모델로 탑재하고, Claude Sonnet도 지원하는 Cursor/Windsurf의 경쟁 제품이에요.

그러면 질문이 완전히 달라지죠. Claude Code와 AntiGravity는 **같은 범주의 경쟁 도구**예요 — 둘 다 agentic IDE. 그래서 "자율실행 체계를 AntiGravity에 적용"한다는 건 이전 대화의 3계층 아키텍처와 문서 전략을 **Claude Code에서 AntiGravity로 이식**하는 문제예요.

---

## 같은 범주, 다른 구현 — 진짜 차이점

AntiGravity는 파일 트리 대신 Agent Manager가 첫 화면으로 등장하는 agent-first 플랫폼이에요. 개발자가 고수준 목표를 선언하면 에이전트 인스턴스들이 병렬로 각 태스크를 처리하고, UI에서 각 스트림의 상태와 산출물을 모니터링하는 구조예요.

모델은 Gemini 3 Pro가 기본이고 Claude Sonnet 4.5(thinking 포함)와 GPT-OSS 120B도 지원해요. 비용은 현재 공개 프리뷰 기간 중 무료이며, 5시간마다 rate limit이 리셋돼요.

에디터 뷰와 에이전트 뷰 두 가지 인터페이스를 제공해요 — 직접 코딩이 필요할 때는 기존 AI IDE처럼 쓰고, 자율실행이 필요할 때는 에이전트에게 태스크를 위임하는 방식이에요.

---

## Claude Code 체계 → AntiGravity 이식 매핑

핵심은 **파일 기반 컨텍스트(`CLAUDE.md`, `TASK.md`)가 AntiGravity에서도 동일하게 작동한다**는 거예요. 둘 다 로컬 파일시스템에 접근하는 VS Code 기반이니까요. 차이는 두 곳에서 생겨요.

첫째, **컨텍스트 주입 방식**이에요. Claude Code는 `CLAUDE.md`를 자동으로 읽지만, AntiGravity는 Knowledge Items라는 개념으로 에이전트가 유용한 컨텍스트와 코드 스니펫을 knowledge base에 저장해서 미래 태스크에 참조하는 방식을 써요. 즉 `CLAUDE.md`의 역할을 Knowledge Items가 흡수해요. 기존 `CLAUDE.md` 내용을 Knowledge Item으로 등록해두면 이식 완료예요.

둘째, **에스컬레이션 UX**예요. Review-driven development 모드를 권장 설정으로 제시하는데, 에이전트가 결정을 내리고 사람 승인을 요청하는 방식이에요. Claude Code의 터미널 인터럽트 패턴과 개념은 같지만 UI가 더 시각화되어 있어요.

셋째, **멀티에이전트**예요. Agent Manager를 통해 여러 에이전트를 서로 다른 워크스페이스에서 비동기적으로 병렬 실행할 수 있어요. Claude Code가 단일 세션 순차 실행이라면 AntiGravity는 병렬 팩토리에 더 가까워요. 이게 CSP의 "24시간 팩토리" 목표에 구조적으로 더 잘 맞는 부분이에요.

---

## 실용적 결론

`TASK.md`, `CONTEXT.md`, `DOD.md` 같은 문서 체계는 **그대로 이식 가능**해요. 파일시스템 기반이 동일하니까요. 달라지는 건 `CLAUDE.md` → Knowledge Items 등록, 단일 실행 루프 → Agent Manager 병렬 분배, 터미널 인터럽트 → Review-driven 승인 UI 정도예요.

AntiGravity의 Agent Manager 병렬 실행을 EX Intelligence, ESCON, Pulse Check 세 프로젝트에 동시 적용하는 구체적 설계로 들어가볼까요?

chat_messages.text: claude.md 는 agent.md로 작성하면 된다는 이야기로 해석하면 되나?

content.text: claude.md 는 agent.md로 작성하면 된다는 이야기로 해석하면 되나?

chat_messages.text:
좋은 질문인데, 단순히 파일 이름만 바꾸는 문제는 아니에요. 조금 더 확인해볼게요.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

정확하게 파악됐어요. 단순히 이름만 바꾸는 게 아니라 훨씬 구조적인 얘기예요.

결론부터 말하면 — **"CLAUDE.md = AGENTS.md"는 맞지만, AntiGravity에서는 더 정교한 3단 계층이 있어요.**

AntiGravity v1.20.3부터 `AGENTS.md`를 공식 지원하는데, 이건 Antigravity, Cursor, Claude Code가 모두 읽는 **cross-tool 공용 규칙 파일**이에요. 프로젝트 루트에 `AGENTS.md`를 두면 어느 AI 도구든 자동으로 인식해요. 그러니까 CSP가 지금 Claude Code용으로 쓰는 `CLAUDE.md`를 `AGENTS.md`로 바꾸면 AntiGravity에서도 즉시 작동해요.

그런데 여기서 멈추면 AntiGravity 고유의 장점을 절반만 쓰는 거예요. 실제 파일 계층은 이렇게 구성돼요.

---

```
프로젝트 루트/
│
├── AGENTS.md              ← Claude Code + AntiGravity 공용 (cross-tool)
│                            "나는 누구고 무엇을 해야 하는가"
│
├── .agent/
│   ├── rules/             ← AntiGravity 전용 Rules
│   │   └── ex-intelligence.md   항상 로딩되는 프로젝트 제약
│   │
│   ├── workflows/         ← AntiGravity 전용 Workflows  
│   │   └── build-portal.md      /build-portal 로 트리거
│   │
│   └── skills/            ← AntiGravity 전용 Skills
│       └── lg-cho-portal/ SKILL.md + 템플릿 에셋
│
└── ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md    ← 전역 규칙 (모든 프로젝트에 적용)
```

---

Rules는 에이전트의 "system instruction" 혹은 불변 헌법이에요. 사용자가 트리거하지 않아도 항상 켜져 있는 패시브 가이드라인이에요. 이게 Claude Code의 `CLAUDE.md`와 가장 유사한 역할이에요.

Workflows는 Rules와 달리 능동적 절차예요 — `/build-portal` 같은 명령어로 직접 트리거하는 반복 가능한 태스크 플레이북이에요. Claude Code에는 이 개념이 없어요. 이게 AntiGravity만의 추가 레이어예요.

Skills는 항상 로딩되는 게 아니라, 요청 내용이 skill 설명과 매칭될 때만 컨텍스트에 로드되는 Progressive Disclosure 방식이에요. CSP가 이미 `lg-cho-portal-builder` 스킬을 Claude Code용으로 만들어뒀는데, 이걸 `.agent/skills/` 아래에 그대로 이식하면 AntiGravity에서도 동작해요.

---

## 정리하면 이렇게 매핑돼요

| Claude Code | AntiGravity | 비고 |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE.md` | `AGENTS.md` | cross-tool 공용, 이름만 변경 |
| — | `.agent/rules/` | 프로젝트 제약, AntiGravity 전용 |
| — | `.agent/workflows/` | 반복 태스크 플레이북, 신규 개념 |
| `/mnt/skills/` | `.agent/skills/` | 구조 동일, 경로만 다름 |
| `TASK.md` | `TASK.md` | 그대로 사용 가능 |

만약 CSP가 Antigravity만 쓴다면 `GEMINI.md`만 있으면 충분하지만, Claude Code도 함께 쓰는 듀얼 환경이라면 공용 규칙은 `AGENTS.md`에, AntiGravity 전용 오버라이드는 `GEMINI.md`에 분리하는 게 권장 패턴이에요. 충돌 시 `GEMINI.md`가 우선순위를 가져요.

CSP의 현재 Mac(Claude Code) + AntiGravity 듀얼 환경에 딱 맞는 구조예요. `CLAUDE.md` → `AGENTS.md`로 rename하고, AntiGravity 전용 오버라이드는 `GEMINI.md`에 추가하면 두 환경이 하나의 소스에서 돌아가요.

content.title: Getting Started with Google Antigravity | Google Codelabs
content.text:
While Antigravity's underlying models (like Gemini) are powerful generalists, they don't know your specific project context or team standards . Loading every single rule or tool into the agent's context window leads to "tool bloat", higher costs, latency and confusion. Antigravity Skills solve this through Progressive Disclosure. A Skill is a specialized package of knowledge that sits dormant until needed. It is only loaded into the agent's context when your specific request matches the skill's description . Skills are directory-based packages. You can define them in two scopes depending on your needs: Global Scope (~/.gemini/antigravity/skills/): Available across all your projects (e.g., "Format JSON", "General Code Review") . Workspace Scope (<workspace-root>/.agents/skills/): Available only within a specific project (e.g., "Deploy to this app's staging", "Generate boilerplate for this specific framework") . ... my-skill/ ├── SKILL.md #(Required) metadata & instructions.

Rules serve as the “system instructions” or the immutable constitution for the agent. They are passive, persistent guidelines that the agent must consider before generating any code or plan.

Welcome to the tutorial on Antigravity, Google’s free and experimental Agent first development platform. There is a codelab version of this tutorial too. If you’d like to follow step by step, check it out. It is now available as a 2-part codelab: ... On a side note, I write a bi-weekly newsletter that delivers key Google Cloud Platform news and announcements you need to know, to your inbox. Subscribe now. ... November 28, 2025: - How to navigate this tutorial. - Workflows and Rules in Antigravity - Agent Security — Allow List, Deny List and Browser Allow Lists. ... You can think of Antigravity as a new agentic development platform that evolves the traditional IDE into an agent-first experience. Unlike standard coding assistants that just autocomplete lines, Antigravity provides a "Mission Control" for managing autonomous agents that can plan, code, and even browse the web to help you build.

Rules serve as the “system instructions” or the immutable constitution for the agent. They are passive, persistent guidelines that the agent must consider before generating any code or plan. They are not triggered by the user; they are always “on,” As a result of this, the agent’s output will start aligning with he developer’s intent. Rules function at two distinct scopes, allowing for a hierarchy of control that mirrors software inheritance: Global Rules: These apply to every project you open in Antigravity. They define your personal coding philosophy or organizational mandates. Storage Location: ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md. Use Case: Setting preferences for specific languages (e.g., “Always use TypeScript over JavaScript”), documentation standards (“Always add docstrings”), or ethical guidelines (“Never generate hardcoded API keys”).

Developers require their tools to behave consistently. In a standard LLM chat interface, users often find themselves repeating the same context: “I use Python 3.9,” “Use snake_case,” “Don’t use this library.” · In order to be efficient, we want our tools to understand this, follow the guidelines and help us be more efficient, so that we don’t have to keep repeating this. Antigravity solves this through a dual-layer customization system: Rules and Workflows. Workflows and Rules are configurable from multiple places: You can go to the Editor window and at the bottom right, you will see Antigravity - Settings. Click on that and then Customizations → Manage. You will see the following Rules and Workflows settings, as shown below: ... Alternately, you can be in the Editor Window and the Agent conversation panel and then click on the …

When a user asks an agent to “run a database migration,” the agent scans its available Skills (Global or Workspace specific). If it finds a database-migration skill, it loads those specific instructions and execution protocols into its context window. This allows the agent to perform the task not just correctly in a general sense, but correctly according to the specific standards and safety protocols defined by the engineering team. Skills effectively turn the generalist Gemini model into a specialist. They allow an organization to codify its best practices (security checks, code style preferences, deployment steps) into executable assets that the AI follows rigorously. While Antigravity’s underlying models (like Gemini) are powerful generalists, they don’t know your specific project context or team standards . Loading every single rule or tool into the agent’s context window leads to “tool bloat”, higher costs, latency and confusion.

In the context of Google Antigravity, a Skill is a directory-based package containing a definition file (SKILL.md) and optional supporting assets (scripts, references, templates). It is a mechanism for on-demand capability extension. On-Demand: Unlike a System Prompt (which is always loaded), a Skill is only loaded into the agent’s context when the agent determines it is relevant to the user’s current request. This optimizes the context window and prevents the agent from being distracted by irrelevant instructions. In large projects with dozens of tools, this selective loading is crucial for performance and reasoning accuracy.

content.title: Advanced Tips for Mastering Google Antigravity | Amulya Bhatia
content.text:
They serve distinct purposes: Rules are passive constraints (context injected into the model), while Workflows are active procedures (repeatable tasks triggered by you). Here is the detailed breakdown of how each works, where they live, and ...

In Antigravity, Rules and Workflows are the two primary mechanisms for customizing agent behavior. They serve distinct purposes: Rules are passive constraints (context injected into the model), while Workflows are active procedures (repeatable tasks triggered by you). Here is the detailed breakdown of how each works, where they live, and how to configure them. Rules are persistent instructions that “guide” the agent’s thinking. They act like a System Prompt that is always running in the background. You use Rules to enforce coding standards, architectural patterns, or project-specific constraints without having to repeat them in every chat message. Activation: They are applied automatically based on their configuration (e.g., always on, or only for specific file types). Format: Standard Markdown (.md) files. ... Workspace Rules: Stored in .agent/rules/ within your project root / workspace folder.

Manual: You must explicitly reference it (e.g., @security-rule) to activate it. Workflows are Saved Prompts or “Playbooks” that you trigger on demand. They are designed for complex, multi-step tasks that you perform frequently, such as “Review this PR,” “Generate Unit Tests,” or “Refactor this module.” · Activation: Triggered manually by typing / followed by the workflow name in the chat (e.g., /test). Format: Markdown (.md) files containing a sequence of instructions. ... Workspace Workflows: Stored in .agent/workflows/ within your project / workspace root. Global Workflows: Stored in ~/.gemini/antigravity/global_workflows/. A workflow file usually looks like a numbered list of steps. The agent executes them sequentially. You can even chain workflows (e.g., a “Ship Feature” workflow that calls a “Run Tests” workflow). When you type /review in the chat, the agent executes this: We are used to reading code diffs.

If Antigravity’s agents are the brains of your IDE, MCP servers are its “nerves” out into the rest of your tools and infrastructure. MCP (Model Context Protocol, originally from Anthropic) is quickly becoming the USB-C of AI apps: a standard way for AI agents in IDEs (VS Code, JetBrains, Antigravity, etc.) to talk to external tools like databases, SaaS APIs, file systems, and more. In Antigravity, MCP servers show up as extra tools and resources the agent can call: think “ask Firebase to deploy this,” “query Power BI,” “talk to Supabase,” “use Qdrant as a vector store,” or “drive Blender from natural language.” · Each MCP server exposes one or more tools behind a consistent protocol: Tools – things the agent can do: run queries, send emails, call APIs, manipulate files, trigger deployments, etc. Resources – external data the agent can read, like documents, dashboards, or datasets.

Prefer official or well-known servers (e.g., Firebase, Databricks, Qdrant, Context-7) that are listed in the MCP server registry. Start with conservative review and terminal policies so the agent has to ask before using dangerous tools or commands. For internal MCP servers, keep credentials in environment variables or secret managers, not hard-coded in config. Used thoughtfully, MCP effectively lets you say: “Antigravity, you’re allowed to operate these tools on my behalf” — and then your prompts and workflows become much more powerful. Under the hood, Antigravity’s intelligence comes from large AI models – notably Google’s Gemini 3 Pro and others like Anthropic’s Claude Sonnet / Opus 4.5. As an advanced user, you should be mindful of which model you’re using for which task, and how to manage the resource constraints: Choose the Right Model per Task: Antigravity lets you choose from multiple models for each agent conversation.

정확하게 파악됐어요. 단순히 이름만 바꾸는 게 아니라 훨씬 구조적인 얘기예요.

결론부터 말하면 — **"CLAUDE.md = AGENTS.md"는 맞지만, AntiGravity에서는 더 정교한 3단 계층이 있어요.**

AntiGravity v1.20.3부터 `AGENTS.md`를 공식 지원하는데, 이건 Antigravity, Cursor, Claude Code가 모두 읽는 **cross-tool 공용 규칙 파일**이에요. 프로젝트 루트에 `AGENTS.md`를 두면 어느 AI 도구든 자동으로 인식해요. 그러니까 CSP가 지금 Claude Code용으로 쓰는 `CLAUDE.md`를 `AGENTS.md`로 바꾸면 AntiGravity에서도 즉시 작동해요.

그런데 여기서 멈추면 AntiGravity 고유의 장점을 절반만 쓰는 거예요. 실제 파일 계층은 이렇게 구성돼요.

---

```
프로젝트 루트/
│
├── AGENTS.md              ← Claude Code + AntiGravity 공용 (cross-tool)
│                            "나는 누구고 무엇을 해야 하는가"
│
├── .agent/
│   ├── rules/             ← AntiGravity 전용 Rules
│   │   └── ex-intelligence.md   항상 로딩되는 프로젝트 제약
│   │
│   ├── workflows/         ← AntiGravity 전용 Workflows  
│   │   └── build-portal.md      /build-portal 로 트리거
│   │
│   └── skills/            ← AntiGravity 전용 Skills
│       └── lg-cho-portal/ SKILL.md + 템플릿 에셋
│
└── ~/.gemini/GEMINI.md    ← 전역 규칙 (모든 프로젝트에 적용)
```

---

Rules는 에이전트의 "system instruction" 혹은 불변 헌법이에요. 사용자가 트리거하지 않아도 항상 켜져 있는 패시브 가이드라인이에요. 이게 Claude Code의 `CLAUDE.md`와 가장 유사한 역할이에요.

Workflows는 Rules와 달리 능동적 절차예요 — `/build-portal` 같은 명령어로 직접 트리거하는 반복 가능한 태스크 플레이북이에요. Claude Code에는 이 개념이 없어요. 이게 AntiGravity만의 추가 레이어예요.

Skills는 항상 로딩되는 게 아니라, 요청 내용이 skill 설명과 매칭될 때만 컨텍스트에 로드되는 Progressive Disclosure 방식이에요. CSP가 이미 `lg-cho-portal-builder` 스킬을 Claude Code용으로 만들어뒀는데, 이걸 `.agent/skills/` 아래에 그대로 이식하면 AntiGravity에서도 동작해요.

---

## 정리하면 이렇게 매핑돼요

| Claude Code | AntiGravity | 비고 |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE.md` | `AGENTS.md` | cross-tool 공용, 이름만 변경 |
| — | `.agent/rules/` | 프로젝트 제약, AntiGravity 전용 |
| — | `.agent/workflows/` | 반복 태스크 플레이북, 신규 개념 |
| `/mnt/skills/` | `.agent/skills/` | 구조 동일, 경로만 다름 |
| `TASK.md` | `TASK.md` | 그대로 사용 가능 |

만약 CSP가 Antigravity만 쓴다면 `GEMINI.md`만 있으면 충분하지만, Claude Code도 함께 쓰는 듀얼 환경이라면 공용 규칙은 `AGENTS.md`에, AntiGravity 전용 오버라이드는 `GEMINI.md`에 분리하는 게 권장 패턴이에요. 충돌 시 `GEMINI.md`가 우선순위를 가져요.

CSP의 현재 Mac(Claude Code) + AntiGravity 듀얼 환경에 딱 맞는 구조예요. `CLAUDE.md` → `AGENTS.md`로 rename하고, AntiGravity 전용 오버라이드는 `GEMINI.md`에 추가하면 두 환경이 하나의 소스에서 돌아가요.

chat_messages.text: AntiGravity의 Agent Manager 병렬 실행을 EX Intelligence, ESCON, Pulse Check 세 프로젝트에 동시 적용하는 구체적 설계로 들어가자

content.text: AntiGravity의 Agent Manager 병렬 실행을 EX Intelligence, ESCON, Pulse Check 세 프로젝트에 동시 적용하는 구체적 설계로 들어가자

chat_messages.text:
좋아요. 실제 설계로 들어가기 전에 AntiGravity Agent Manager의 병렬 실행 구조를 한 번 더 확인할게요.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

구조가 명확하게 파악됐어요. 이제 CSP의 세 프로젝트에 맞는 실제 설계로 바로 들어갈게요.

핵심 전제를 먼저 짚고 가면 — 워크스페이스당 에이전트 하나가 권장 패턴이에요. 에이전트 간 충돌을 방지하기 위해 각 워크스페이스는 독립적인 컨텍스트를 유지해야 해요. 즉 EX Intelligence, ESCON, Pulse Check는 각각 별도 워크스페이스 폴더로 분리되어야 해요. 그리고 2026년 초 업데이트로 Claude Code 스킬 표준이 AntiGravity에 도입되어 Claude Code용으로 만든 스킬이 수정 없이 AntiGravity에서 실행돼요 — CSP의 `lg-cho-portal-builder` 스킬이 그대로 이식되는 거예요.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

좋아요. 전체 구조를 먼저 조감하고, 각 프로젝트의 실제 파일까지 내려가볼게요.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

전체 그림이 나왔어요. 이제 각 워크스페이스의 실제 파일 구조와 핵심 설정 내용으로 내려갈게요.

---

## 폴더 구조 — 한 번에 세 프로젝트

```
~/hr-workspace/
├── AGENTS.md                     ← 세 프로젝트 공용 헌법
│
├── ex-intelligence/              ← WS1
│   ├── AGENTS.md                 ← EX Intelligence 전용 override
│   ├── TASK.md                   ← Agent A 실시간 실행 로그
│   ├── .agent/
│   │   ├── rules/ex-rules.md
│   │   ├── workflows/build-portal.md
│   │   └── skills/lg-cho-portal/  ← 기존 스킬 그대로 이식
│   └── outputs/
│
├── escon/                        ← WS2
│   ├── AGENTS.md
│   ├── TASK.md
│   ├── .agent/
│   │   ├── rules/escon-rules.md
│   │   ├── workflows/deploy-vercel.md
│   │   └── skills/skill-ontology/
│   └── src/
│
└── pulse-check/                  ← WS3
    ├── AGENTS.md
    ├── TASK.md
    ├── .agent/
    │   ├── rules/pulse-rules.md
    │   ├── workflows/run-survey.md
    │   └── skills/pulse-survey/
    └── forms/
```

---

## 세 AGENTS.md의 핵심 내용

각 프로젝트 AGENTS.md는 구조는 동일하되 내용이 달라요. CSP가 자리를 비워도 에이전트가 스스로 판단할 수 있도록 세 가지만 선언하면 돼요.

**WS1 — EX Intelligence** (`ex-intelligence/AGENTS.md`):

```markdown
# EX Intelligence Agent Charter

## 역할
LG CHO부문 HR 포털 HTML 파일 생성 및 유지보수.
lg-cho-portal-builder 스킬을 우선 사용한다.

## DoD (완료 조건)
- [ ] HTML 파일이 /outputs에 저장됨
- [ ] 브라우저에서 오류 없이 열림
- [ ] LG 브랜드 컬러(#A50034) 적용 확인

## 에스컬레이션 조건 (반드시 CSP 승인 필요)
- 기존 outputs 파일 덮어쓰기
- 외부 CDN 신규 추가
- 디자인 시스템 변경

## 금지 사항
- GEMINI.md 또는 루트 AGENTS.md 수정 금지
- /outputs 외 경로에 최종 파일 저장 금지
```

**WS2 — ESCON** (`escon/AGENTS.md`):

```markdown
# ESCON Agent Charter

## 역할
Next.js 14 + TypeScript + Supabase 기반
Engineering Skill Competency Navigator 개발.
Vercel 배포 전 로컬 빌드 검증 필수.

## DoD
- [ ] `npm run build` 오류 없음
- [ ] Supabase 타입 생성 완료
- [ ] 변경사항 git commit

## 에스컬레이션 조건
- DB 스키마 변경 (valid_from/valid_to 컬럼)
- Vercel 배포 실행
- 외부 API 키 필요

## 기술 제약
- Node.js 18+ 유지
- any 타입 사용 금지
- D3.js 버전 고정 (현재 v7)
```

**WS3 — Pulse Check** (`pulse-check/AGENTS.md`):

```markdown
# Pulse Check Agent Charter

## 역할
분기 조직 건강도 설문 시스템.
Supabase Magic Link 인증 + 동의 기반 참여 추적.

## DoD
- [ ] 설문 폼 HTML 생성 완료
- [ ] Supabase 연결 테스트 통과
- [ ] 개인정보 동의 플로우 포함

## 에스컬레이션 조건
- 실제 직원 데이터 접근
- 설문 문항 내용 변경 (HR 정책 관련)
- 이메일 발송 실행

## 모델 우선순위
반복적 폼 생성 → Gemini Flash
로직 설계 판단 → Claude Sonnet
```

---

## 세 Workflow 파일 — 진짜 자율실행의 핵심

Workflows는 `/` 명령으로 트리거하는 반복 가능한 플레이북이에요. CSP가 Mission Control에서 `/build-portal`, `/deploy`, `/run-survey`를 치는 순간 각 에이전트가 정해진 절차를 자율 실행해요.

```markdown
# .agent/workflows/build-portal.md  (EX Intelligence)
## /build-portal

1. TASK.md 읽고 현재 진행 상태 확인
2. lg-cho-portal-builder SKILL.md 로드
3. 최신 요구사항 반영하여 HTML 생성
4. 브라우저 에이전트로 로컬 검증 실행
5. DoD 체크리스트 자가 검증
6. /outputs에 저장
7. TASK.md 업데이트 후 Artifact 생성
8. CSP에게 완료 보고 (Inbox)
```

```markdown
# .agent/workflows/deploy-vercel.md  (ESCON)
## /deploy

1. TASK.md 확인 — 에스컬레이션 조건 체크
2. npm run build 실행 → 오류 시 자동 수정 시도
3. Supabase 타입 재생성
4. git add . && git commit -m "[auto] build verified"
5. ⚠️ Vercel 배포는 CSP 승인 후 실행 (에스컬레이션)
6. TASK.md 업데이트
```

```markdown
# .agent/workflows/run-survey.md  (Pulse Check)
## /run-survey

1. 설문 폼 HTML 생성 (pulse-survey skill 로드)
2. Supabase 연결 테스트
3. Magic Link 인증 플로우 검증
4. 브라우저 에이전트로 E2E 테스트
5. ⚠️ 실제 발송은 CSP 승인 필수
6. 테스트 결과 Artifact 생성
```

---

## 모델 배분 전략

각 워크스페이스별로 다른 모델을 지정하는 게 가능한지 커뮤니티에서도 논의되고 있어요. 현재 가능한 최선의 배분은 작업 성격 기반이에요.

EX Intelligence는 창작적 판단(디자인, 문서)이 많아 Claude Sonnet 4.6. ESCON은 코드 추론과 대형 컨텍스트(TypeScript 코드베이스 전체)가 필요해서 Gemini 3 Pro. Pulse Check는 반복적인 폼 생성과 테스트 실행이 주라서 Gemini Flash로 비용을 아껴요.

---

## CSP의 하루 루틴 — 실제로 어떻게 쓰는가

아침에 Mission Control을 열면 세 에이전트의 Inbox가 보여요. 에이전트들이 생성한 Artifact — 태스크 리스트, 구현 계획, 스크린샷, 브라우저 녹화 — 를 훑어보고, 문제가 있으면 Artifact에 직접 코멘트를 달면 에이전트가 실행을 멈추지 않고 반영해요.

승인이 필요한 에스컬레이션 항목(Vercel 배포, 파일 덮어쓰기, 실제 이메일 발송)만 CSP가 결정하면 돼요. 나머지는 세 에이전트가 병렬로 돌고 있어요. 이게 "24시간 팩토리"가 실제로 작동하는 모습이에요.

각 프로젝트 AGENTS.md를 실제 파일로 완성해드릴까요, 아니면 특정 워크플로우 파일을 더 구체적으로 짜볼까요?

content.text: 좋아요. 실제 설계로 들어가기 전에 AntiGravity Agent Manager의 병렬 실행 구조를 한 번 더 확인할게요.

content.title: Getting Started with Google Antigravity | Google Codelabs
content.text:
In Antigravity's Manager View, a developer can dispatch five different agents to work on five different bugs simultaneously, effectively multiplying their throughput. If you click on Next above, you have the option to open a workspace. Think of workspace as you knew from VS Code and you will ...

Upon launching Antigravity, the user is typically greeted not by a file tree, but by the Agent Manager, as shown below: This interface acts as a Mission Control dashboard. It is designed for high-level orchestration, allowing developers to spawn, monitor, and interact with multiple agents operating asynchronously across different workspaces or tasks. In this view, the developer acts as an architect. They define high-level objectives, examples could be: ... As the diagram above indicates, each of these requests spawns a dedicated agent instance. The UI provides a visualization of these parallel work streams, displaying the status of each agent, the Artifacts they have produced (plans, results, diffs), and any pending requests for human approval. This architecture addresses a key limitation of previous IDEs that had more of a chatbot experience, which were linear and synchronous.

In Antigravity's Manager View, a developer can dispatch five different agents to work on five different bugs simultaneously, effectively multiplying their throughput. If you click on Next above, you have the option to open a workspace. Think of workspace as you knew from VS Code and you will be done. So we can open up a local folder by clicking on the button and then selecting a folder to start with. In my case, I had a folder in my home folder named my-agy-projects and I selected that. You can use a completely different folder. Note, you can completely skip this step if you'd like and you can open up a workspace at any time later too. Once you complete this step, you will be in the Agent Manager window, which is shown below: You will notice that the application is immediately geared to start a new conversation in the workspace folder (my-agy-projects) that was selected.

Let's spend a bit of time now on the Agent Manager (window) here and understand a few things, so that it's clear about the basic building blocks, how you navigate in Antigravity and more. The Agent Manager window is produced below: Please refer to the above diagram with the numbers: Start Conversation: Click on this to begin a new conversation. This will directly lead you to the input where it says Ask anything. Workspaces: We mentioned about Workspaces and that you can work across any workspace that you want. You can add more workspaces at any time and can select any workspace while starting the conversation. Playground: This is a great way by which you can simply start a conversation with the agent and then if you'd like to convert that into a workspace, where you have stricter control over the files, etc. Think of this as a scratch area. Browser: One of the clear differentiators that makes Antigravity very powerful and that is its close integration with the Chrome browser.

Editor View: You can switch at any time to the editor view. This will show you your workspace folder and any files generated. You can directly edit the files there, or even provide inline guidance, command in the editor, so that the Agent can do something or change as per your modified recommendations/instructions. We will cover the editor view in detail in a later section. As per the documentation, when the agent wants to interact with the browser, it invokes a browser subagent to handle the task at hand. The browser subagent runs a model specialized to operate on the pages that are open within the Antigravity-managed browser, which is different from the model you selected for the main agent. This subagent has access to a variety of tools that are necessary to control your browser, including clicking, scrolling, typing, reading console logs, and more.

Workspaces: This section allows you to interact with multiple workspaces and agents working within them. Ideally, you’d have one agent per workspace to avoid conflicts between agents.

Parallel agents in Antigravity Google Antigravity transforms your regular IDE into an agentic development platform. In my previous blog posts, I showed some of the unique features of Antigravity …

Editor is powerful and familiar, but what sets Antigravity apart is the Agent Manager. Agent Manager, as the name suggests, is a higher-level view into the work agents are doing under your guidance. You can work across multiple workspaces, oversee multiple agents simultaneously, and interact with your codebase primarily through the agent, rather than by writing code directly. As agents and models continue to improve, this bird’s-eye view will become the primary entry point for all your work. Let’s take a look at a concrete example of how the Agent Manager is useful. When you first open the Agent Manager, you’re greeted with this view: ... Inbox: This is where you can keep track of the latest messages from each conversation with the agents. It helps you to stay updated on the latest approvals or feedback needed from your side. Start conversation: This is where you can initiate a conversation with an agent in a workspace.

At any point, I can switch to the editor by selecting Focus Editor from the workspace: In atamel.dev, I can see that the blog post file and the date have been updated: In genai-beyond-basics, a new README.md has been created explaining the samples: In genai-samples, the code is updated and a new unit test project has been created by Antigravity: ... Although the unit tests failed initially, I got them working after a few iterations through the chat. Great! In this blog post, we explored how to run multiple agents across multiple workspaces. While I personally prefer to handle one task at a time and I’ll most likely stick to the editor, it’s good to know that Antigravity offers a higher-level way of working across workspaces. For more information, check out the documentation and our codelabs: ... Originally published at https://atamel.dev. ... A collection of technical articles and blogs published or curated by Google Cloud Developer Advocates.

Workspaces: This section allows you to interact with multiple workspaces and agents working within them. Ideally, you’d have one agent per workspace to avoid conflicts between agents. However, it’s still possible to have multiple agents working on the same workspace. Playground: This is an independent workspace for quick prototypes that does not require importing a full workspace. You can move playgrounds into a workspace later. Let’s now start some agents to work on workspaces in parallel. As you can see, I have added three of my workspaces to Agent Manager: atamel.dev: Contains the code for my personal blog. I have a blog post scheduled for January 19th; however, I want to publish it later, so I need to change the file name and the publish date within the blog post. genai-beyond-basics: This repo contains some A2A and MCP samples in the protocols folder.

content.title: Build with Google Antigravity, our new agentic development platform - Google Developers Blog
content.text:
It’s a dedicated interface where you can spawn, orchestrate, and observe multiple agents working asynchronously across different workspaces. Antigravity allows you to offload end-to-end tasks that previously required constant context switching.

We built Antigravity because we believe agents shouldn't just be chatbots in a sidebar; they should have their own dedicated space to work. The platform introduces two distinct ways to interact with your code: The Editor View: When you need to be hands-on, you get a state-of-the-art, AI-powered IDE equipped with tab completions and inline commands for the synchronous workflow you already know. The Manager Surface: This is where the shift happens. It’s a dedicated interface where you can spawn, orchestrate, and observe multiple agents working asynchronously across different workspaces. Antigravity allows you to offload end-to-end tasks that previously required constant context switching.

Delegating work to an agent requires trust, but scrolling through raw tool calls is tedious. Antigravity solves this by having agents generate Artifacts—tangible deliverables like task lists, implementation plans, screenshots, and browser recordings. These Artifacts allow you to verify the agent's logic at a glance. If something looks off, you can leave feedback directly on the Artifact—similar to commenting on a doc—and the agent will incorporate your input without stopping its execution flow. We want Antigravity to be your home base for the era of agents. It treats learning as a core primitive, allowing agents to save useful context and code snippets to a knowledge base to improve future tasks. Google Antigravity is available today in public preview, at no cost for individuals. This cross-platform solution is compatible with MacOS, Windows, and Linux, offering model optionality with generous rate limits on Gemini 3 Pro, and full support for Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4.5 and OpenAI's 

Download at antigravity.google/download and experience liftoff.

content.title: Google Antigravity Guide: Multi-Agent Platform 2026
content.text:
Assign multiple agents to different workspaces within a single project. One agent handles frontend development while another builds backend APIs and a third writes tests. Each agent maintains independent context, preventing conflicts and allowing ...

Antigravity flips this relationship. You act as the project manager, defining goals and requirements, while AI agents become your development team executing the actual work. This agent-first approach works particularly well for full-stack development, rapid prototyping, and complex multi-component systems where coordinating different parts of a project traditionally requires significant mental overhead. The Mission Control interface serves as your command center for managing multiple AI agents. Instead of juggling different windows and contexts, you get a unified inbox where all agent communications flow, making it easy to track what each agent is doing and coordinate their activities. Through Mission Control, you can assign different agents to separate workspaces, allowing parallel development on frontend, backend, database, and testing tasks simultaneously.

For founders and product teams needing quick proof-of-concepts, Google Antigravity accelerates MVP development dramatically. Specify your core features, and agents build functional prototypes in hours rather than days or weeks. Request “Create a fitness tracking mobile app using Flutter with workout logging, progress charts, and cloud sync” using Fast Mode with Gemini 3 Flash, and watch as the agent generates the complete application including UI, business logic, backend APIs, and deployment configuration. Assign multiple agents to different workspaces within a single project. One agent handles frontend development while another builds backend APIs and a third writes tests. Each agent maintains independent context, preventing conflicts and allowing true parallel development. Coordinate agents through the unified inbox in Mission Control, where you can review progress from all agents, provide feedback, and adjust priorities as the project evolves.

Download the installer from the official website at antigravity.google and select the version matching your operating system. For Mac users, choose between Apple Silicon and Intel versions based on your hardware. Run the installer like any standard application. On first launch, you’ll be asked to sign in with your Google account, select your preferred AI model (Gemini 3 Flash, Claude Opus 4.5, or others) from the available options, and configure terminal permissions. You can import your existing VS Code settings if you’re migrating from another editor, or start with default configurations. The entire setup process takes less than five minutes, and you’ll immediately have access to the full platform with all supported AI models and generous rate limits during the preview period. Start by creating a new workspace in the Agent Manager panel. Click “New Workspace,” name your project, and select a folder where files should be stored.

Google Antigravity provides a ready-to-use platform where agent orchestration happens through natural language, making it accessible to developers who want agentic workflows without building infrastructure from scratch. Developers interested in understanding AI agent development at a deeper level might explore both approaches—using the platform for rapid development while studying frameworks like LangChain for foundational knowledge. Windsurf emphasizes team collaboration with its Cascade AI system that coordinates multiple agents across team members. It includes sophisticated workspace management, code review automation, and features designed specifically for development teams. Mission Control offers robust multi-agent management but targets individual developers or small teams. Windsurf makes sense for agencies and larger development teams that need advanced collaboration features, while Google Antigravity suits solo developers and smaller projects prioritizing autonomous agent capab

content.title: Mastering the Antigravity Agent Manager: 2026 Guide (Part 1)
content.text:
In 2026, Google Antigravity has changed from a simple tool for writing code into a "Mission Control" for AI workers. Most users fail because they treat it like a chatbot, leading to "Instruction Drift" and wasted tokens.

Unlock the full power of Google Antigravity. Learn to use the Agent Manager for parallel sub-agents, global rules, and visual logic verification in 2026.

In 2026, Google Antigravity has changed from a simple tool for writing code into a "Mission Control" for AI workers. Most users fail because they treat it like a chatbot, leading to "Instruction Drift" and wasted tokens. To win, you must adopt an Agent-First Workflow: organize your work by giving each website its own folder. Build a "Skills Library" using simple .md files so your AI knows exactly what to do. By leveraging the Agent Manager and Inbox, you can orchestrate multi-threaded builds where parallel sub-agents research, code and test simultaneously. Mastering these foundational "Infinity Stones" ensures that your AI remains a coherent, high-performing virtual CTO rather than a hallucinating intern. Fact: Antigravity agents use Artifacts (implementation plans, task lists and browser recordings) as a "Trust Layer", allowing you to verify logic without reading every line of code.

The fix is simple: one task, one conversation window. When a task is complete, click + to open a fresh conversation. You can always navigate back to old conversations through Antigravity's history, so you never lose context, you just don't drag it forward unnecessarily. When the main agent is struggling, you have 3 fallback options that most users never discover: ... Double-click the middle editor pane and the Claude plugin appears (if installed). This gives you a clean, isolated context and you can spawn multiple instances of it simultaneously. This option is great for running parallel tasks. ... Click Terminal → New Terminal → type claude to launch a terminal-based interface. Open as many of these as needed, each with their own independent context. You can think of them as separate brains you can consult at the same time. ... Right-click any panel to reposition it (left, right, top, bottom).

In seconds, the invoice is created, the address is looked up and it's saved to your desktop. With only one sentence, it collapses multi-step task switching into one command. That's the point, Antigravity has access to all your projects and files simultaneously through Agent Manager, so nothing requires switching contexts. For tasks that can run simultaneously, you can open a new terminal and try this: Use the sub-agent feature and spin up five sub-agents, each to research a different [topic] and write a 500-word report. Save each to the left-hand sidebar. Antigravity orchestrates 5 parallel agents, each with their own context window, all running at once. This is multi-threading for knowledge work, tasks that would normally require sequential thinking can be parallelized safely. Research, competitor analysis, content drafts… anything that's repetitive but parallel is a candidate for this.
